column2:
Type

(lack of type indicates no type to be used in TEI)

This is a legacy of our early TEI. We keep this information and keep encoding it in new documents for the sake of consistency. It has no searchability or relevance and wasn't implemented rigorously. What I mean is that categories and labels aren't very good, and because of the sprawl of annotations did not end up being applied consistently.
column4:
Name

This is the title/label of the one liner and should match the text preceding the colon in the write-up itself.
column5:
Write-up

The text preceding the colon in this column should match the text in the name/label column.

On this tab only, follow this format: "Term: definition of geographic place or feature--in other words, WHAT it is. Then, discuss the historical/cultural/geographical importance, including any connection, if relevant, to story of Lone Woman and its key characters. Always include, when relevant, the Native American perspective; e.g., "the homeland of XXX people."
column6:
Sources & Notes

Wherever possible, the information in this column should be in the format of a citation rather than a single link. Full/formal citations are not necessary, but the goal should always be to allow someone else to follow the documentary/digital trail back to the item, whether or not its digital location has changed.

column5: Acapulco: a port city on the Pacific coast of Mexico located in the state of Guerrero.  Although its affluence declined after Mexican independence, it regained importance during the California Gold Rush, becoming a popular destination for ships traveling to and from Panama.

Acuragna: a former Gabrieleno (Tongva) settlement located near Los Angeles, California, although its precise location is unknown.  It was mentioned in a handbook about Native Americans published by the Smithsonian Institution in 1907 and 1910.
Adobe house: this phrase may refer to a building owned by the Santa Cruz Island Wool-growing Company, but it is unclear. The island was used primarily for sheep-ranching at this time.

Africa: the world’s second largest continent, boarded by the Mediterranean Sea, the Indian Ocean, the Suez Canal and Red Sea along the Sinai Peninsula, and the Atlantic Ocean. It is believed that homo sapiens first evolved in Ancient Africa, with hunter-gatherer societies existing as early as 6000 BCE.  Africa is comprised of 54 independent countries today.

column5: Alameda County: a county in California incorporated in 1853. In 1860, Alameda had 465 residents.

Alamos Harbor (Santa Cruz Island): most likely refers to a port on Santa Cruz Island located near Alamos Beach. This area of the island is now part of a larger totality owned by the Nature Conservancy, a nonprofit organization that purchased a considerable portion of Santa Cruz Island in 1978 with the aims of restoring ecological balance to the isle. Much of today's ecological instability can be traced to overgrazing by wild pigs and sheep, products of livestock brought to the site during the nineteenth century.

Alaska: the forty-ninth state of the United States, which joined the union in 1959. First inhabited between 20,000 and 10,000 BCE, the state is home to many indigenous groups, including 229 federally recognized tribes. A Russian expedition first explored the region in the seventeenth century and the state-sponsored Russian American Company, which was granted a monopoly on the fur trade, operated out of Sitka (New Archangel) through the early nineteenth century.  The United States government purchased Alaska from Russia in 1867.

column5: Alcatraz: a rocky island in San Francisco Bay. The island was first used as a military fortification and became the site of the first American lighthouse on the Pacific Ocean in 1854. Alcatraz was the nation’s first official Army prison and, in 1934, became a civilian prison, hosting criminals such as Al Capone, "Machine Gun" Kelly, and Robert Stroud before closing in 1963. American Indian activists occupied the island between 1969-71, attracting considerable attention to the civil rights movement. Today, Alcatraz is part of the Golden Gate National Recreation Area and is a popular tourist destination.
Aleupkigna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located in Santa Anita/Arcadia, California (in Los Angeles Valley).  Rancho Santa Anita was located there.

Aleutian Islands: an archipelago located off the coast of southwest Alaska, including numerous volcanic islands varying in size. The Russians claimed the island chain in the eighteenth century. During the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, the Aleut (or Unangax) people were conscripted into service of the Russian American Company as maritime hunters.

Alta California (Upper California): a polity of the Viceroyalty of New Spain and after Mexican independence, a territory of Mexico.  In 1848, with the end of the Mexican-American War, the land comprising Alta California (present-day California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Wyoming) became part of the United States.  Spain claimed the territory, which was home to many politically and linguistically distinct indigenous peoples, in the sixteenth century, when Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo entered San Diego Bay.  It did not begin colonization efforts until the eighteenth century, however, when the first of what was to become 21 Franciscan missions was constructed under the direction of Junípero Serra.

Alta California: a polity of the Viceroyalty of New Spain and after Mexican independence, a territory of Mexico.  In 1848, with the end of the Mexican-American War, the land comprising Alta California (present-day California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Wyoming) became part of the United States.  Spain claimed the territory, which was home to many politically and linguistically distinct indigenous peoples, in the sixteenth century, when Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo entered San Diego Bay.  It did not begin colonization efforts until the eighteenth century, however, when the first of what was to become 21 Franciscan missions was constructed under the direction of Junípero Serra.

Alta California: a polity of the Viceroyalty of New Spain and after Mexican independence, a territory of Mexico.  In 1848, with the end of the Mexican-American War, the land comprising Alta California (present-day California, Nevada, Utah, and parts of Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, and Wyoming) became part of the United States.  Spain claimed the territory, which was home to many politically and linguistically distinct indigenous peoples, in the sixteenth century, when Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo entered San Diego Bay.  It did not begin colonization efforts until the eighteenth century, however, when the first of what was to become 21 Franciscan missions was constructed under the direction of Junípero Serra.
Altadena, California: an unincorporated area in Los Angeles County.
Alto: a census-designated area in Marin County, California. Also called Blithedale, Alto had a population of 711 as of 2010.

American Fork: refers to either the North Fork or the South Fork of the American River. Many miners settled near the South Fork because gold was found nearby, but the North Fork remained relatively undeveloped due to the difficulty of transporting equipment through the surrounding canyon.

American Hotel: a hotel in Panama City, Panama, where American travelers stayed while crossing the Panama Canal. Many guests were appalled by the lack of modern conveniences in the hotel, such as restroom facilities. In 1850, the rate was $2 a day.

American River: a river in California that flows through Sacramento, Placer, and El Dorado counties. The Nisanan people lived on this land for thousands of years before miners arrived in the mid-1800s.

Anacapa Island: a volcanic island comprised of three smaller islets located off the southern coast of California and the closest Southern California Channel Island to the mainland. Used as fishing and hunting grounds by the Chumash, it was first documented by Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo in 1542. During the nineteenth century, the island was used for sheep grazing. Anacapa is currently the most visited island of the Channel Islands National Park.

column5: Anacapa Street: a street in Santa Barbara, California, that points in the direction of Anacapa Island, an island now part of the Channel Islands National Park. Anacapa is derived from the Chumash name Anypakh, which means a deception in appearance or a mirage.
Andover: a town approximately twenty-three miles north of Boston. It is known for being the home of Phillips Academy, a prestigious preparatory school, which also houses a Museum of Archaeology.

Antarctica: the southernmost continent on Earth, Antarctica has never supported sustained human habitation. Since the 1820s, many countries have established year-round and seasonal outposts there for scientific research, but it has continued to be a place relatively isolated from civilization.

Argentina: a country comprising most of southern South America. Originally home to many groups of indigenous people, most notably the Diaguita and Guarani, Argentina was first settled by the Spanish in 1536. The country declared its independence from Spain in 1816, and Argentine nationals were instrumental in similar revolutions in several other South American countries. A military junta took control of the country in 1976, but democracy was restored after the failure of the Falklands Islands War of 1982.

Arizona: the forty-eighth state of the United States, which it joined in 1912. Its borders include the homelands of many Native people, including the Navajo and Hopi. Native resistance to land incursions were effectively suppressed by 1886, paving the way for white settlement.
Arkansas River: the sixth largest river in the United States and a tributary of the Mississippi River. It begins near Leadville, Colorado, and goes through the Rockies and Great Plains.

Arkansas: the twenty-fifth state of the United States, which it joined in 1836. Arkansas is the home territory of the Quapaw, Osage, Caddo, and Tunica peoples. Before the Civil War, the majority of Arkansas settlers were small-scale farmers.

Arlington: a luxury hotel located on State Street in Santa Barbara, California. Though the original hotel burned down in 1909, a second hotel was built in 1911, which was badly damaged by an earthquake in 1925. The hotel was later torn down and replaced by the Arlington Theater, which opened in 1931.
Arroyo Burro: a six-mile stream that begins at the head of Barger Canyon and travels to the Santa Barbara Channel. It is also known as San Roque Creek.
Arroyo Seco: watershed in Southern California that flows from the San Gabriel Mountains to Los Angeles. It is part of the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) people.

Arroyo Sequit: stream in the Santa Monica mountains of Los Angeles County that travels towards the Pacific Ocean. Variant spellings of this place include Arroyo Sequet, Arroyo Siquet, and Arroyo Siquis.

Ashland: a city located in northern Wisconsin on Lake Superior. The area is part of the Ojibwa homeland. French traders arrived around 1659 and a Jesuit mission was founded in 1665. The town of Ashland was settled in 1854 and incorporated in 1887.

Asia: a central destination of the maritime fur trade during the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. Canton, China was the principal point of entry for California furs, as well as many other Pacific goods.

column5: Asucsagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement located at Azuca in Los Angeles County, California. Its more common name is Azucsagna, as it was called by the Smithsonian Bureau of Ethnology.

Austin's Grant: refers to the area of land in Texas granted to Moses Austin (and later his son, Stephen). With the permission of the government of the eastern portion of the Provincias Internas, 300 families settled this area in 1821.

Awigna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement near present-day La Puente in the San Gabriel Valley of Los Angeles County, California. Its name has many possible variations, including Ahwingna, Awigana, and Awizna.

Baja California (Lower California): a province of the Viceroyalty of New Spain and after Mexican independence, a territory of Mexico.  Its land included the Baja California Peninsula.  Seventeen missions to convert the indigenous people of the peninsula were founded by the Jesuits and then, in the late eighteenth century, taken over by the Dominican order. Today, Baja California refers to two states in the Republic of Mexico, Baja California Norte and Baja California Sur.

Bangkok: the capital and primary cultural center of Thailand located on the Chao Phraya River. In 1782, Rama I moved the royal court to the east bank of the Chao Phraya, making Bangkok the capital of the country. In 1971, the city on the east bank (Krung Thep) and the city on the west bank (Thon Buri) were united, merging into the larger city of Krung Thep Maha Nakhon (Bangkok Metropolis).
Barrow: the northernmost city in the United States. Barrow is located on the Chukchi Sea coast of Alaska. Its traditional name is Ukpeagvik, meaning "the place where snowy owls are hunted."

Bear River: the longest river in the western hemisphere never reaching the ocean. The Bear River passes through Idaho, Utah, and Wyoming and is the largest tributary to the Great Salt Lake. The original inhabitants of the River Valley were the Fremont (700-1300 CE) and Shoshone, but French-Canadian trappers arrived in the early 1800s, and settlers arrived by 1864.

Begg’s Rock: a 15-foot-tall rock located eight miles northwest of San Nicolas Island. It is named after the John Begg, a ship that hit a nearby rock in 1824. Today, Begg Rock is a popular scuba diving destination.

Belgium: a country in Western Europe bordered by the North Sea, France, and the Netherlands. It did not gain its independence from the Netherlands until 1830 and was invaded by Germany during World War I and II.

Benicia: a town founded in 1847 and located in California near the Carquinez Strait. Several schools were established in Benicia during the 1850s, which led it to be referred to as the "Athens of California." It was the state capital from 1853-54.

Berkeley: a city on California's East Bay.  Berkeley is home to the University of California's flagship campus, one of the nation's premier universities.  In 2016, Berkeley had a population of approximately 100,000 people, nearly 40,000 of whom were enrolled in the university.

Berlin: the capital and royal residence of Prussia beginning in 1709 under King Friedrich I. During the nineteenth century, industrialism contributed to rapid growth in the city; by 1871, Berlin and its suburbs had over 900,000 inhabitants.
Big Dog Cave: the site of important archaeological discoveries on San Clemente Island, including the remains of human and animal burials.
Billerica: a town twenty-four miles northwest of Boston. Until the 1850s, the town had approximately 10,000 residents and an agriculturally-based economy.

Biltmore: a luxury hotel in Santa Barbara, California, that opened in 1927. Originally part of the Biltmore Hotels group, it is now owned by the Four Seasons. The Coral Casino, an elite social club, was founded at the hotel in 1937.

Black Cave: a fictional cave on the Island of the Blue Dolphins in Scott O'Dell's 1960 novel. Here, Karana discovers the remains of her ancestors, though the significance of this place in her people's history has been forgotten.

Black Point: the headquarters for the commandant of nearby Fort Point located in the San Francisco Bay area. Today, it is incorporated with the area of Green Point and remains a relatively rural area, with a population of 1,306 in 2010.

Bodega: area located in Sonoma County, California. The Bodega Bay area was first inhabited by the Miwok and Pomo peoples. Russian fur traders, who constructed Fort Ross twenty-four miles north of the bay, were the first European settlers in the region.

Boston: Massachusetts city founded in 1630. Due to its deep harbor and convenient location, it became a large center of sea-based commerce, becoming the richest and largest city in the American colonies. The New England port was so central to the Northwest Trade of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries, in which otter pelts loomed large, that in parts of the Pacific Northwest, all American seamen, regardless of their home ports, became known as "Boston men."
Botany Bay: located in Sydney, Australia, and originally named Stingray Harbour by James Cook in 1770. The British planned to use this site as their first penal colony to exile prisoners.
Botany Bay: located in Sydney, Australia, and originally named Stingray Harbour by James Cook in 1770. The British planned to use this site as their first penal colony to exile prisoners.

Branch's Ranch: 17,000-acre ranch located eight miles from Pismo Beach in the central coast area of California. The ranch belonged to Francis Ziba Branch, a sea otter hunter who requested a land grant from the Mexican government in 1837.

column5: Bridgeport, Connecticut: a city located in Connecticut on the Long Island Sound. Traditionally occupied by the Pequot, the area was first settled by Europeans in 1639. Bridgeport was incorporated as a city in 1836. P. T. Barnum, the famous showman, was a resident of Bridgeport and served as its mayor from 1875-76.

British line: refers to the international boundary line that divides the continental United States and Canada. Historically, this line was extremely contentious (particularly in the western part of the United States) because many Americans questioned the legitimacy of British surveying methods. This resulted in land disputes which were settled by treaties.

Bueno Porto Island: refers to Santa Catarina Island off the coast of Brazil. Bueno Porto was a creek on the island in which ships could anchor to find respite from heavy winds. The explorer Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo had a shelter here.

Buncombe County: a county in western North Carolina; Asheville is the county seat. Although originally the home of the Cherokee, settlers named it after Revolutionary War hero Col. Edward Buncombe. Established in 1792, the county's boundaries included most of western North Carolina, where about 1,000 people lived. By 1850, its economy was mostly based on the drover trade (driving animals from the West to market). Today, the county is a major tourist center due to its location in the Appalachian Mountains.

Burton Mound: originally a vibrant Chumash village, the area became part of Mission Santa Barbara during the Spanish period. Following secularization of the missions, the land was eventually sold to Joseph Chapman, who built an adobe that was occupied by George Nidever and his family during the 1840s. 1851, Nidever sold the home to Augustus F. Hinchman, who in turn sold it to Lewis T. Burton, whose name became associated with the site. During the early twentieth century, a hotel was built on the property, but it burned to the ground in 1921. At this point, archaeological excavation was conducted in the area under the direction of John P. Harrington. Today, a plaque notes marks Burton Mound as California Registered Historical Landmark No. 306.

Bush Street: a street in San Francisco, California where a musical hall and theater were located. At this musical hall, the Lone Woman's dress was displayed as part of a curiosity exhibit in 1853. A fire erupted on the street in 1877 but did not cause much damage.

Butte County: a county in northern California that was part of the homeland of the Maidu. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after Marysville or Sutter Buttes, which were within the county boundaries when established. The county seat is located in Oroville, and the county had a population of 224,301 in 2016.
Cabeugna: a former Tongva (Gabrielino) village located near the San Fernando Mission in California (its precise location is unknown). Popular variations of the name include Cahuenga.
Cahuenga: a former Tongva (Gabrielino) village located near the San Fernando Mission in California (its precise location is unknown). Popular variations of the name include Cabuenga.

Caire rancho: extensive ranch on Santa Cruz Island named after Justinian Caire, who acquired all the holdings of the Santa Cruz Island Company in the 1880s. Caire built up the Company's holdings and diversified operations to include ranching as well as carpentry, blacksmithing, and construction facilities. Until the late 1930s, the Caire family owned the island and continued ranching operations.

Calaveras County: a county in Northern California and the homeland of the Miwok. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850 and is named after the Calaveras River, which an explorer reportedly named due to the large quantity of Indian skulls found on the river’s banks. The county seat is located in San Andreas, and the county had a population of 44,881 in 2016.

Calcutta: a city in western India now known as Kolkata. Calcutta, which is an Anglicized form of the Bengali word Kalikata, was the capital of British India from 1772 to 1911. Today, Kolkata is the capital of West Bengal and one of the most populated and overcrowded cities in India.

California Channel Islands: eight islands that lie west of the California coast around Santa Barbara, Ventura, and Los Angeles. The Channel Islands (Anacapa, San Clemente, San Miguel, San Nicolas, Santa Barbara, Santa Catalina, Santa Cruz, and Santa Rosa) were home to the Chumash (northern islands) and Gabrielino (Tongva) (southern islands) peoples for thousands of years before the arrival of Europeans. After the establishment of the Spanish mission system, the Channel Islands were gradually depopulated, both as a result of Spanish removal policies and due to migration following the disruption of traditional lifeways. The islands were variously used for sheep grazing, cattle ranching, and commercial and recreational fishing. William Wrigley, Jr., of chewing gum fame, also developed Santa Catalina as a destination for tourists in the 1920s. Today, San Nicolas and San Clemente Islands are controlled by the US Navy, Santa Catalina, which has a resident population of approximately 4,000, is largely administered by the Catalina Nature Conservancy, and the other five islands are protected as part of the Channel Islands National Park.

California Desert: a desert area in the territory of the Mojaves, Chemehuevis, and Quechans, who occupied the land until about 1868. At this point, the desert land filled with military bases, utility roads and lines, ranches, and mining sites. In 1976, a 25-million-acre region of the California Desert was designated by Congress as the California Desert Conservation Area.

California: the thirty-first state to join the union, on September 9, 1850. The land within its borders was annexed from Mexico after the Mexican-American War. When California became a US state, slavery was prohibited as part of the Compromise of 1850. Today, California has the largest population of any state in the union.

Callao: a port city on the coast of Peru. Founded in 1537 by Francisco Pizarro, it was also the site of Spain’s 1826 surrender in the Peruvian War of Independence. Callao was a popular stop for ships engaged in the China trade in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries; most of the cargo taken aboard consisted of gold and silver seized from the Inca Empire by Spanish conquistadores.

column5: Cape Horn: the southernmost point of South America, located at the tip of the Isla Hornos off Chile. "Rounding the Horn" by ship is both difficult and dangerous, requiring great skill.

Carpinteria: a city that, during the pre-contact period, was home to the Chumash and an active trading site. In 1769, members of the Portolà expedition, the first European exploration of what is now California, noted the native population's use of redwood canoes and called the place a "carpentry" shop.
Casa de Olive: the home of Carl Dittman, located on State Street in Santa Barbara, California.  Dittman bought the house in 1850 from the estate of a ship captain, James Scott.

Cave of the Whales: sea cave on the south coast of San Nicolas Island named for the petroglyphs, or cave paintings, found inside. Some figures prominently feature the kind of dorsal fins found on whales or sharks.

Cedros Island: an island in the Pacific Ocean approximately fourteen miles from Baja California. Also known as Cerros Island, the island was originally occupied by Cochimí-speaking people. The Spanish first visited the island in 1539 and transported the Natives to the mainland of Baja California in 1732. Today the island is home to a fishing village established in the 1920s.

Central America: the region between Mexico and South America consisting of Panama, Costa Rica, Nicaragua, Honduras, El Salvador, Guatemala, and Belize. The Mayan civilization flourished in the region from 250 to 900 CE. The Spanish first arrived in 1502, when Christopher Columbus sailed near Panama. The Spaniards colonized Central America by splitting it into viceroyalties, and many Native peoples died due to disease and labor exploitation. The colonial period ended in 1821, when Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Costa Rica, and Nicaragua all became independent nations. In the twentieth century, the region was politically dominated by dictators, and its economy was increasingly reliant on exporting coffee and bananas.

Cerros Island: an island in the Pacific Ocean approximately fourteen miles from Baja California. Also known as Cedros Island, the island was originally occupied by Cochimí-speaking people. The Spanish first visited the island in 1539 and transported the Natives to the mainland of Baja California in 1732. Today the island is home to a fishing village established in the 1920s.

Chester: a city in Pennsylvania located between Philadelphia and Wilmington, Delaware. Originally settled by Europeans in 1645, it is the oldest town in Pennsylvania. Due to its location on the Delaware River, there were five shipyards nearby, including the one in which the Santa Rosa steamer was built.

Chetko: likely refers to the Chetco River, a river in Curry County, Oregon. It begins in the Kalmiopsis Wilderness area of the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest and travels 55.5 miles before emptying into the Pacific Ocean between the towns of Harbor and Brookings.

Chiapas: a state in southern Mexico that borders Guatemala. Chiapas contains a large population of indigenous peoples, most notably those of Mayan descent. Although Chiapas was connected with Guatemala during Spanish colonization, it became a state of Mexico in 1824. Due to the socioeconomic inequality in the region, a large armed uprising began in 1994 and lasted into the twenty-first century.

China: one of the largest nations in the world, occupying the greater part of East Asia and 3,705,405 square miles of land. China also has one of oldest records of continuous civilization, dating back 4,000 years. Technologies linked to Chinese civilization include silk production, which began as early as 1300 BCE, and a lunisolar calendar system dating to 500 BCE. In the thirteenth century, the Venetian Marco Polo traveled across China on the Silk Road, establishing a relationship with the Mongol leader Kublai Khan. Through his writings, Marco Polo sparked Europe’s interest in the region. However, it was not until the nineteenth century that Chinese interaction with Europe increased as Western military powers forced the nation to open its doors to trade and to Christian missionaries. China became a central destination for maritime furs and abalone meat from California.

Chinese Harbor: a stretch of sand that lies on the northwest coast of San Nicolas Island. Its name stems from its use by nineteenth-century Chinese abalone harvesters.  It is also called Corral (or Coral) Harbor.
Chokisgna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Bell Gardens, California. Also known as Chibugna, it is mentioned in the San Fernando Mission registry.
Chowigna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Palos Verdes, California. Human occupation dates back at least 8,000 years.
Cimarron: a tributary of the Gunnison River in Colorado. The Cimarron flows through Montrose and Gunnison counties before reaching the Gunnison River at Black Canyon in Gunnison National Park.

Claremont: a city on the eastern edge of Los Angeles County, California, it is part of the homeland of the Serrano people. Much of the city’s land was incorporated into the San Gabriel Mission in the late eighteenth century, and became the site of Rancho San Jose in the nineteenth century. By the early twentieth century, Claremont saw the establishment of several prestigious colleges, collectively known as the Claremont Colleges, and a growing citrus industry that included four citrus packing and distribution houses.

Clear Creek: refers to either Clear Creek, a 5.25-mile-long tributary of the Steely Fork Cosumnes River in El Dorado County, California, or Clear Creek, an eight-mile-long tributary of the Cosumnes River’s north fork in El Dorado County, California.

Coast Ranges: mountains that extend along two-thirds of California’s coast, from Humboldt County in the north to Santa Barbara County in the south. The northern and southern ranges are divided by San Francisco Bay, with the tallest mountains located in the southern region.

Coches Prietos: an anchorage on the south-central coast of Santa Cruz Island. It was the site of the largest Chumash village on Santa Cruz, Liyam. Now encompassed by the Channel Islands National Park, Coches Prietos is a popular access point for visitors because of its wide, calm beach and proximity to hiking trails.
