column5: New Mexico: the forty-seventh state of the United States, which it joined on January 6, 1912. New Mexico is the home of the Apache, Comanche, Jocome, Jano, Pueblo, Navajo, Ute, and Zuni people. Today, its primary industries are mining, oil and natural gas extraction, and tourism.

New Orleans: a city in southeastern Louisiana near the mouth of the Mississippi River. The New Orleans area was originally inhabited by Woodland and Mississippian people. In 1718, the governor of French Louisiana founded Nouvelle-Orléans. In 1762-63, Louisiana was ceded to Spain, and New Orleans was controlled by the Spanish until 1803, when it reverted back to French rule and was sold to the United States twenty days later as part of the Louisiana Purchase. In 2005, the city was devastated by Hurricane Katrina, breaching four levees and flooding 80% of the city.

New Spain: Imperial Spain’s empire, which in the eighteenth century was the largest in the world. The Spanish Age of Empire began with the union of the Catholic Kings Ferdinand and Isabella of Aragon and Castile and was strengthened by the Inter caetera, the papal bull of 1493, and the Treaty of Tordesillas (1493), in which the Pope divided the globe between the Spanish and Portuguese. When Mexico was under the control of the Spanish crown, it stretched from Central America as far north as San Francisco Bay in California.

New York City: the most populous city in the United States, located in the state of New York and encompassing five boroughs: Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, the Bronx, and Staten Island. Originally home of the Lenape, New York City was first settled by Europeans in 1624, when the Dutch West India Company sent approximately thirty families to live in a settlement they called New Amsterdam on Governor’s Island. In 1626, they moved to Manhattan Island, and the city grew rapidly. By 1810, the city was the most populous in the Western Hemisphere, housing more than 202,589 residents. Over 8 million people lived in New York City in 2016.

New Zealand: an island country in the South Pacific Ocean comprised of two main islands, the North and South Islands, and several smaller islands. The country is remote, lying over 1,000 miles away from its closest neighbor, Australia. Inhabited by the Maori people, who first settled the island around 800 CE, New Zealand was annexed by Great Britain in 1840. It became independent from Britain in 1947, but it remains part of the Commonwealth.

Newport: a city located on Aquidneck Island, Rhode Island, and part of the territory of the Wampanoag and Narragansett peoples. Founded in 1639, the city boasted a thriving seaport during the colonial era. After the American Civil War, the city became a vacation destination for America’s wealthiest citizens. In 2010, the city had a population of 24,672.

Nicaugna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at a place later referred to as Carpenter's Ranch in Los Angeles County. This land includes the present-day cities of Santa Fe Springs and Downey.

Nice: a resort city in France on the French Riviera located near the Italian border on the Mediterranean Sea. The city was founded by Phocaeans around 350 BCE. In 1860, the city was ceded to France. Today, it is a major tourist destination.

Nimitopal: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. Also referred to as Nimitapal, the Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade. Though the Chumash did not have contact with Europeans until the sixteenth century, by 1822, all Chumash people had been relocated to the mainland.

Nipomo Ranch: a 37,888-acre Mexican land grant located in San Luis Obispo County, California. It was given to Captain William G. Dana in 1837 and used as a popular stop for travelers. In 1847, it became one of four exchange locations on the state’s first mail route.

Niquipos: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. The Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade. While the Chumash did not have contact with Europeans until the sixteenth century, by 1822, all Chumash people had been relocated to the mainland.

Nitel: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. The Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade. Although the Chumash did not have contact with Europeans until the sixteenth century, by 1822, all Chumash people had been relocated to the mainland. Some scholars suspect that the town may have also been called Swahul.

Nome Cult: refers to the Nome Cult Farm, an extension of the Nome Lackee Indian Reservation. The farm was established in 1856 in Mendocino County, California, because a government agent recommended that the Nome Lacka resettle at a reservation at Nome Cult. After Nome Lackee Reservation was attacked in 1861, the remaining residents moved to Nome Cult, which had been converted into a reservation named Round Valley.

Nome Lakee Indian Reservation: reservation established in Tehama County, California in 1854 as a result of the Indian Appropriation Act of 1852. In 1861, the reservation was attacked by the Knights of the Golden Circle, a secessionist group, and this virtually destroyed the reservation. The Native people remaining on the reservation were moved to Nome Cult Farm, and the national government began to sell the land of Nome Lackee in 1870.

Normandy Coast: a part of the northern coast of France that faces the English Channel. The Normandy Channel Islands (Guernsey, Sark, Jersey, and Alderney) are located to the west of the Normandy Coast. Normandy is best known for being the site of the Allied invasion during D-Day in World War II.

Northern Channel Islands: another name for the Santa Barbara group comprised of Anacapa, San Miguel, Santa Cruz, and Santa Rosa Islands, which lie west of the Santa Barbara Channel. The Chumash, a maritime people, lived on the larger three islands—San Miguel, Santa Cruz, and Santa Rosa—until brought to the mainland by the Spanish.

Oahu: the third-largest island of Hawaii and the site of the state capital, Honolulu. Oahu was first settled by Tahitians in 500 CE. and was conquered by Hawaiian King Kamehameha I in 1795. In 1845, Oahu’s Honolulu became the royal capital. In the early twentieth century, Oahu became the site of the US’s primary naval base in the Pacific, Pearl Harbor, which was attacked by the Japanese in 1941, bringing the United States into World War II.

Oceanside: a city in the San Luis Rey Valley of California. Originally inhabited by the Payomkowishum, the area was first settled by Europeans when the Mission San Luis Rey was founded in 1798. The mission was secularized in 1834; it fell into disrepair and settlers moved into the valley in the following decades. The city of Oceanside was incorporated in 1888, and it had an estimated population of 174,558 in 2013.

Ohio: the seventeenth state of the United States, which it joined on March 1, 1803. Ohio is the homeland of the Erie, Kickapoo, and Shawnee people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, mining, and manufacturing.

Oklahoma: the forty-sixth state of the United States, which it joined on November 16, 1907. Oklahoma is the home of the Plains Apache, Arapaho, Caddo, Comanche, Kiowa, Osage, and Wichita people. Today, its primary industries are agriculture and oil and natural gas extraction.

Old Chapel: Mission Santa Barbara went through several different chapels during its operation: the first was built in 1788, the second in 1789, the third in 1794, and the fourth in 1820. The chapel is home to an eighteenth-century Chumash altar encrusted with abalone shells.

Old Garden Spring: a spring located on Redeye Beach on the northwestern side of San Nicolas Island in California. This spring was located near the Lone Woman’s camp and is probably where Nidever’s group found the Lone Woman’s basket in 1852.

Old Presidio: a military outpost founded by the Spanish (and forcibly constructed by the Chumash) in 1782. It served as protection and the seat of government for nearby settlers until 1846 when Americans took possession of the then-decrepit fort. Today, the presidio is part of a state historic park in downtown Santa Barbara.

Oregon: the thirty-third state of the United States, which it joined on February 14, 1859. The Alsea, Cayuse, Chetco, Chinook, Clatskanie, Coos, Galice, Applegate, Kalapuya, Klamath, Modoc, Molala, Multnomah, Nez Perce, Paiute, Shasta, Siuslaw, Takelma, Tillamook, Tolowa, Tututni, Coquille, Umatilla, Umpqua, Walla Walla, Wasco, and Wishram people call Oregon home. Today, the state's primary industries are agriculture, lumber, paper production, mining, and technology.

Oroville: the county seat of Butte County, California, incorporated in 1906. Located on the banks of the Feather River, Oroville was a center for mining during the Gold Rush. Its most famous resident was Ishi, a Yahi man who emerged from hiding in 1911 and was deemed the "last of his tribe." Ishi was studied by scientists at UC-Berkeley for the remaining five years of his life.
Ortega Hill: a 253-foot-tall summit in Santa Barbara County, California.

Painted Cave: a large sea cave on the northwestern part of Santa Cruz Island named for the colorful rocks, lichens, and algae it contains. The cave is 100 feet wide and almost a quarter of a mile long.

Palo Alto: a city located on the shore of San Francisco Bay between San Jose and San Francisco. Palo Alto was developed around Stanford University beginning in 1891, and, three years later, the city was incorporated. Palo Alto is considered by many to be the birthplace of Silicon Valley because Hewlett-Packard began here.

Palos Verdes: a peninsula on the Pacific coastline, southwest of Los Angeles. During the early nineteenth century and during Scott O’Dell’s childhood, the peninsula was largely undeveloped. It was later used for ranching and is now a thriving residential and commercial area.

Panama: a country located on the Isthmus of Panama in Central America. It is home to the Kuna and Chocó people, but became the first Spanish colony on the Pacific Ocean. In the mid to late eighteenth century, Panama served as a staging point for people traveling to California, especially during the Gold Rush. Completion of the Panama Canal, in 1914, greatly expedited travel. The United States did not relinquish control of the canal until 1999.

Paris: the capital of France, located on the river Seine in north-central France. The area around Paris was first inhabited around 7600 BCE and then incorporated by the Romans, Gauls, and Franks. The city was significantly impacted by the French Revolution, which began with the storming of the Bastille in 1789. From 1940 to 1944, Paris was occupied by Nazi Germany, a crushing defeat for the French during World War II. In 1968, Paris was rocked by a series of student protests that focused on Paris’ social and economic issues, hastening the country's modernization.

Park Hotel: a hotel located on the corner of State and Montecito streets in Santa Barbara, California. Constructed in 1854, the two-story brick house was originally the home of Isaac J. Sparks, Santa Barbara’s third mayor.

Pasadena Playhouse: a community theater located in Pasadena, California. The theatre was founded in 1917-18 after the Gilmor Brown Players settled in the area. The acting troupe obtained the 700-seat building in 1925, and the theater thrived until the 1950s. The Pasadena Playhouse was forced to close in the 1960s but reopened in 1986.

Pasadena: a city in Los Angeles County, California. Pasadena is part of the homeland of the Hahamogna (Tongva) people. Mission San Gabriel was established nearby in 1771. Pasadena was founded in 1874, named in 1875, and incorporated in 1886. Today, the city is famous for being home to the Rose Bowl, California Institute of Technology, and the Jet Propulsion Laboratory.
Pascegna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near the San Fernando Mission. Variant spellings include Pasecgna, Pasheeknga, and Pasheekngna.
Passinogna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located in the Chino Hills of San Bernardino County, California. Other names include Pasino, Pasinogna, and Passinogna.
Pátzcuaro: the first capital city of the Tarascan empire. Pátzcuaro is located on the shores of Lake Pátzcuaro in Michoacán (west-central Mexico). The capital was later relocated to Tzintzuntzan.

Petaluma Creek: formerly a creek in the marshes above San Pablo Bay, California. In 1959, the creek was dubbed a river by an act of Congress so it would qualify for dredging. Today, the river flows through Petaluma into the San Pablo Bay, making the area easier to navigate by boat.

Phoenix: the capital of Arizona, located in the Valley of the Sun in south-central Arizona. The area around Phoenix was occupied by the Hohokam from approximately 700 CE to 1400 CE; they were succeeded by the Akimel O’odham. In 1868, the town of Phoenix was founded by American settlers who claimed that the new town rose from the ruins of a former civilization, like the mythical bird. When Arizona became a state in 1912, Phoenix became the state’s capital. Phoenix has grown steadily into the twenty-first century, with the Phoenix-Mesa-Glendale Metro Area containing a population of 4,192,887 in 2010.
Piniocagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at the Ybarra Ranch near Los Angeles, California. Variant spellings include Pimokanga and Pimocagna.

Pismo Rancho: an 8,838-acre Mexican land grant located in San Luis Obispo County, California. It was given to Jose Ortega in 1840 and later acquired by Isaac Sparks. The rancho’s lands included modern-day Shell Beach, Pismo Beach, and Grover Beach.

Pistol River: a river that travels through Curry County, Oregon, for thirty-two kilometers before emptying into the Pacific Ocean fifteen miles south of Gold Beach. Pioneer James Mace allegedly lost his pistol in the river, giving the stream its name.

Pit River: a tributary of the Sacramento River. It travels for 207 miles through Shasta, Lassen, and Modoc counties before joining the Sacramento in Shasta Lake. The Pit and Klamath rivers are the only rivers in the western United States that begin in the Great Basin and flow to the Pacific Ocean.

Placer County: a county in northern California whose land is the home of the Maidu and Miwok people. It was incorporated on April 25, 1851, and its name likely derives from a contraction of plaza de oro, a Spanish phrase that means "the place of gold." The county seat is located in the city of Auburn, and the county had a population of 371,558 in 2016.

Platte River: a tributary of the Missouri that begins in Lincoln County, Nebraska, and travels 310 miles to Plattsmouth, where it joins the Missouri River. The river’s valley, which is wide and flat, was a major part of both the Oregon and Mormon trails on which settlers moved west in the nineteenth century.

Point Año Nuevo: now a state park on the central California coast, Point Año Nuevo is part of the homeland of the Quiroste, a group of Ohlone people. The Spanish came to the area in 1769, and in 1791, Mission Santa Cruz began to exploit the region’s resources and incorporate the Ohlone people into the mission. Beginning in 1842, after the secularization of the mission system, most of the land comprising what is now Año Nuevo State Park was used for private ranchos and farms . The State of California bought the land in 1971. Today, the 4,000-acre park is a popular site for viewing a variety of land and marine wildlife.

Point Arguello: a cape located between Rocky Point and Point Pednernales in Santa Barbara County, California (Chumash territory). It was named to honor the Spanish colonial governor Don José Dario Arguello in 1793. The United States Navy established a facility for missile testing here in 1945.

Point Conception: a point off the California coast between Santa Barbara and San Luis Obispo, California. One of the many places named by explorer Sebastián Vizcaíno, Point Conception was the site of one of several lighthouses constructed along the California coast in the mid-nineteenth century; the lighthouse is still functioning today.

Point Hueneme: a point off the California coast approximately midway between Ventura and Santa Monica, California. Long a point of departure and rest for Chumash fishermen, a commercial port was built at Point Hueneme in the late nineteenth century. It remains a distribution hub today.

Point Mugu: a point off the California coast between Ventura and Santa Monica, located in Point Mugu State Park. Thought to be one of the sites visited by the explorer Cabrillo, Point Mugu was an active spot for fishing and sporting until the United States Navy acquired the area during World War II. It is now home to Naval Base Ventura County, the Naval Air Warfare Center Weapons Division, and the Naval Satellite Operations Center.  San Nicolas Island is under its jurisdiction.

Point Reyes: a cape located in Marin County, California; it also refers to the Point Reyes Peninsula. The area is part of the homeland of the Coast Miwok people. In 1579, Sir Francis Drake landed here, becoming the first European to visit the area. Today, it is a popular tourist destination.
Point Rincon: a point off the California coast at Rincon Beach State Park, approximately midway between Santa Barbara and Ventura, California.

Point Sal: a cape in northern Santa Barbara County, California, five miles from Guadalupe. Today, the promontory is home to sea lions, deer, and mussels. Many endangered and threatened species, such as the peregrine falcon, visit the point.

Point San Pedro: a port now part of the Port of Los Angeles. Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo arrived here in 1542 and the site was used as a trading post by the Spanish. It was annexed by Los Angeles in 1909 and now boasts one of the world’s largest manmade harbors. This area is part of the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) people.

Point St. George: the third westernmost point in the continental United States, on the northern California coast, and part of the homeland of the Tolowa people. The Point’s lighthouse, the St. George Reef Lighthouse, is the most expensive lighthouse ever built in the US; its construction in 1892 cost $700,000.

Point Vicente: a point off the California coast on the Palos Verdes Peninsula, southwest of Los Angeles, California. A lighthouse was constructed here in 1926 while the rest of the peninsula experienced rapid development. The lighthouse is now run by the United States Coast Guard.

Pomona: a city in Los Angeles County, California. Traditionally the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) people, the area became part of the Rancho San Jose in the 1700s. The city was founded in 1875 and officially incorporated in 1888. In the latter half of the twentieth century, Pomona grew rapidly as the Los Angeles metropolitan area expanded.

Port of San Pedro: the main unit of the Port of Los Angeles, although San Pedro was formerly a separate city. Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo first explored the harbor in 1542 and San Pedro was annexed by Los Angeles in 1909. The harbor's Port of Los Angeles is now the United States' largest port by cargo value and container volume.

Port Orford: a coastal city in Curry County, Oregon. Originally the home of the Qua-toh-mah band of the Tututni people, Port Orford was first settled by people of European descent in 1851, when Captain William Tichenor led a group of settlers to the site. The city was incorporated in 1935, and had a population of approximately 1,200 as of 2016.

Potoltuc: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and later listed in California mission documents. It has also been identified as the Chumash site "Paltuqaq," in Goleta, California.

Prisoners Harbor: a bay on the northern side of Santa Cruz Island. In 1830, approximately thirty convicts were transported to Prisoners Harbor to form a small penal colony. Although their settlement fared well at first, a fire destroyed their camp and, after several months, they were forced to return to the mainland.

Prussia: a historic state in eastern/central Europe. Prussia was originally inhabited by Slavic tribes, which were conquered by the Teutonic knights in 1226. In 1657, Prussia became a hereditary kingdom and later, under King Friedrich II, a great military power that extended its territory beyond present-day Germany. In 1870 (during the Franco-Prussian War), Prussian Prime Minister Otto von Bismarck convinced Germany to unify. The ruler of Prussia became the Emperor of Germany, a position he held until Germany’s defeat in World War I.
Pubugna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near the San Gabriel River and Long Beach, California. Alternate spellings include Pububit, Puvuvit, and Pubu.

Puget Sound: a Pacific estuary, located in the extreme northwest corner of the United States west of Seattle, Washington. The arrival of Europeans and subsequent development of the area in the late eighteenth century decimated Native populations. The area around Puget Sound grew rapidly over the nineteenth century due to its abundant timber, seafood, and petroleum resources. By the twentieth century, the area was one of the most polluted in the country, causing extensive environmental and ecological protections to be placed on it.

Pyramid Head: the southeastern most point (900 feet high) of California's San Clemente Island, which has been controlled by the Navy since 1934 and is closed to the general public. Pyramid Head is a notable landmark in Pyramid Cove because of its prominent, jagged appearance.

Quebec: a province in eastern Canada that lies within Algonquin territory. Jacques Cartier explored the area in 1534 and, seventy-four years later, Samuel de Champlain founded its first settlement. Quebec came under British control after the Seven Years War, joining New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and Ontario in 1867 to form the Dominion of Canada. In the late twentieth century, a powerful separatist movement developed and in 1995, a referendum rejected independence with only a 1% margin of victory.

Rancheria de los Pipimares: a village located on the main road between Los Angeles and San Pedro Harbor also known as the Village of the Island Indians. In the early nineteenth century the area was populated by Channel Island Gabrielinos (Tongva), some of whom had migrated to the mainland from Santa Catalina Island and others who likely migrated from the other Southern Channel Islands, including San Nicolas. The village was located on San Pedro Street between Seventh and Eighth Streets.

Rancho Agua Caliente: a 44,322-acre Mexican land grant in San Diego County, California. Originally home to the Cupeño people, during the Spanish Mission period the land served as a cattle grazing area for Mission San Diego and Mission San Luis Rey. The Cupeño people called a part of this valley Agua Caliente for its hot mineral springs. The first land grant in the valley was given to Silvestre de la Portilla in 1836; Portilla called his land Rancho Valle de San José. In 1840, José Antonio Pico was granted the portion of the valley called Agua Caliente, though he named the land Rancho San José del Valle. Due to trouble with the Cupeño people who did not welcome Spanish rancheros, Rancho San José del Valle and Rancho Valle de San José were soon abandoned. Jonathan Warner, better known as Juan Warner, seeing these ranchos abandoned, asked to have the land and was granted the entire valley in 1844. Warner also had problems with the Natives, and had to rebuild his ranch house after the Cupeño revolted and burned it in 1851. The ranch served as a way station for those traveling to California by the Gila Trail.

Rancho Dos Pueblos: a 2,214-acre ranch in Goleta, California, in the greater Santa Barbara region (Chumash territory).  The ranch was a part of the Santa Barbara Mission system until 1831; in 1842, Nicholas Den became the first private owner of the ranch when he received a Mexican land grant. After the land came under private ownership, it was transformed into a site for large homes and lavish social events.  The land comprising the former ranch is now highly-valued property because it boasts an undeveloped coastline and easy highway access. It was last put on the market in 2009.

Rancho Santa Ana del Chino: part of Juan Bautista Valentín Alvarado y Vallejo’s 22,000-acre land grant; also known as "Chino Ranch." Isaac Williams built an adobe home on this land in 1841, and it eventually became a stop for immigrants coming west to California.

Rattlesnake Island: an island in San Pedro Bay on the coast of southern California. The sandy stretch of land earned its name in the early nineteenth century due to its multitude of rattlesnakes. In 1877, as part of harbor infrastructure improvement, a long breakwater of rocks was built between Rattlesnake Island and close-by Deadman’s Island to carve out a deep shipping channel. The breakwater caused sedimentation and weathering that changed the island’s shape gradually but significantly. In 1891, the Terminal Company bought the island and changed its name to Terminal Island. Author Scott O’Dell was born here in 1898.

Redlands: a city located in San Bernardino County, California, and part of the Serrano people’s homeland. Redlands was founded in 1881 and became an important location for the citrus industry. The city was incorporated in 1888.

Republic of California: an independent republic that existed for a month following the 1846 Bear Flag Revolt between Americans residing in Mexican California and the Mexican government. Following conflict between Mexican and United States forces, the United States briefly assumed control of the territory in July 1846. California would permanently become a part of the United States in 1848, at the end of the Mexican-American War.

Republic of Mexico: a country formed out of New Spain when it won its independence in 1821. Following independence, colonial trade restrictions were lifted, the Catholic missions were secularized, and land grants increased dramatically. Mexico lost almost half of its northern territory, including Alta California, to the United States following the Mexican-American War. The Mexican economy and government stabilized in the 1870s as communication, infrastructure, textiles, and mining flourished, and agriculture rapidly modernized.

River Oaks: a residential community in Houston, Texas built in the 1920s by William and Michael Hoggs. Today, it is famous for being the wealthiest community in Texas. River Oaks was also utilized as a model for effective community planning in the decades after it was built.

Rochester: a city located in Monroe County, New York, known as the country’s first "boomtown" due to the wheat industry and its location on the Erie Canal. Part of the homeland of the Seneca, the village of Rochesterville began in 1817 and was re-chartered as a city with the contemporary name in 1834. In the nineteenth century the city was a thriving home for intellectual and political movements, hailing the likes of Frederick Douglas and Susan B. Anthony. In 2014, Rochester had a population of 209,983.
Rock Crusher: a beach on the western coast of Santa Barbara Island. Rock Crusher is known for its unusual cliffs, which resemble mushrooms due to erosion.

Rockford: a city located in northern Illinois on the Rock River. The area was first used as a burial ground by the Winnebago people. Rockford was founded in 1834 and originally called Midway due to its location halfway between Galena and Chicago. The city was incorporated in 1852 and now serves as the seat of Winnebago County.

Rocky Mountains: a mountain range stretching from Mexico to British Columbia and Alberta (although the northern boundary is disputed). Native peoples in the area include the Shuswap, Kutenai, Coeur d’Alene, Nez Perce, Flathead, Shoshone, Ute, Hopi, Navajo, Blackfoot, Crow, and Cheyenne. Europeans began exploring the Rockies in the sixteenth century. Today, most of the population in the Rockies is concentrated in urban areas near valleys, mountain bases, or railroad lines.

Rogue River: a river that begins at Crater Lake and travels through Oregon for 346 kilometers before emptying into the Pacific Ocean. The Takelma referred to the river as Tak-elam; it played an integral role in Takelma culture and livelihood before they were forcibly relocated to a reservation on the northern coast of the state in the mid-nineteenth century.

Rome: a city located in northwestern Georgia. Rome, which is in Cherokee territory, was settled by European-Americans in 1834 and incorporated in 1847. Rome survived Union occupation in 1864 and a major flood in 1886 to become an important textile production site in the early twentieth century.

Rome: often indicated in place of the Vatican as the destination of several artifacts of the Lone Woman's life. During the mid-nineteenth century, the Vatican was still part of Rome and not considered a sovereign city-state as it is now. Clothing and other items belonged by the Lone Woman may have been sent to the Vatican Museum, a collection begun by the popes in the sixteenth century and organized into separate galleries and museums in the eighteenth century.

Russia: a country located in eastern Europe and northern Asia. During the seventeenth century, Russia expanded into Siberia in pursuit of trade items, including furs. By the eighteenth century, the Russian Empire extended into North America, including Alaska, a rich source of maritime fur (Alaska was ultimately sold to the United States in 1867). Today, Russia is the world’s largest country by land mass. Modern Russia’s origins can be traced back to the Principality of Muscovy, which was founded in the twelfth century. In the early 1600s, the Romanov dynasty was established, and the family ruled until the Russian Revolution of 1917, when Communists led by Vladimir Lenin took control of the country. Russia became the dominant power in the USSR, which was the United States’ primary adversary in the Cold War until 1991, when the Soviet Union collapsed.

Russian America: land encompassing present-day Alaska and northern California (as well as, for a short time, small parts of Hawaii) claimed by the Russian Empire between the early eighteenth century and 1867, when Alaska was sold to the United States. Russia's presence in the region was largely driven by the fur trade, and Native peoples of the Aleutian and Kodiak Islands were conscripted into service hunting sea otter and seals for the state-sponsored Russian American Company (RAC). Alexander Baranov, chief manager of the RAC between 1790-1818, played an outsized role in Russian America's history. After 1818, leadership of the colonial holdings fell to the imperial navy. Russia's legacy can still be seen in the dominance of the Russian Orthodox Church among many Native peoples of Alaska.

Russian River: a California river that travels through Sonoma and Mendocino counties for 110 miles before emptying into the Pacific Ocean. The river is named for the Russians who settled in Sonoma County, at the Russian American Company’s Fort Ross, in 1812. The Russians expanded their settlement of California to include outposts in areas such as Sebastopol, Graton, and Freestone in the early nineteenth century, but abandoned them by 1841.

Sacramento County: a county in northern California that includes the homeland of the Nisenan, Foothills and Southern Maidu, and Valley Miwok and Me-Wuk peoples. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and was named after the Sacramento River by Captain Moraga. The county seat is located in the city of Sacramento, and the county had a population of 1,489,308 in 2016.

Sacramento River: a river that begins near Mount Shasta in northern California and flows 382 miles to Suisun Bay, where it joins the San Joaquin River. Its main tributaries include the Feather, Pit, McCloud, and American Rivers. The river was a major destination for miners in the Gold Rush of 1849.

Sacramento Valley: a valley located in the northern part of the Central Valley in California and part of the homeland of the Miwok and Maidu people. The valley is bordered by the Sierra Nevadas and the Coast Range and is home to the cities of Sacramento and Redding.

Salinas River: a river that begins near the mountains of San Luis Obispo County and flows 170 miles to Monterey Bay, where it empties into the Pacific Ocean. Because the river flows northward, it is nicknamed "the Upside Down River." The Nacimiento River is the main tributary of the Salinas River.

Salinas: a city located in Salinas Valley, California. First populated by the Ohlone (Costanoan) people, the area, which was on El Camino Real, was settled in 1856. The city was incorporated in 1874 and is known as the "Salad Bowl of the World" due to its high agricultural output.
San Antonio: the Spanish name for Silpoponemew, a former Chumash village located approximately four miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

San Bernardino County: a county in northern California that is the homeland of the Serrano people. It was incorporated on April 26, 1853. The county and a nearby mountain are named after Saint Bernard, the patron saint of mountain passes. The county seat is located in the city of San Bernardino, and the county had a population of 2,127,735 in 2016.

San Blas: a port located on the Pacific coast of Mexico, in the state of Nayarit. Founded in 1768, it served as a Spanish naval base used to send supplies to the missions in Alta and Baja California; at its peak, it had a population of just under 30,000. During the second half of the nineteenth century, the Nayarit territory in which San Blas is located became one of the most turbulent areas in Mexico.

San Buenaventura Mission: a Catholic mission located near the Chumash village of Mitsquanaqa'n in the city of Ventura, California. It was founded in 1782. In 1816, at the mission's peak, 1,328 neophytes, most of whom were Chumash, lived here. In 1818, the mission was evacuated for a month due to the threat of a pirate attack. The mission was secularized in 1836 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862.

San Clemente Island: the southernmost of the Southern California Channel Islands. Inhabited by the Gabrielino (Tongva) people, it was first documented by Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo in 1542, and named by Sebastián Vizcaíno in 1602. During the Spanish era, the Natives were removed to missions on the mainland. Since the early 1930s, San Clemente has been used as a United States Navy base.

column5: San Diego County: a county in southern California. Incorporated on February 18, 1850, its name honors the Franciscan San Diego de Alcala de Henares. The county seat is located in the city of San Diego.

San Diego Mission: a mission located in San Diego. Also called San Diego de Alcalá, it was founded in 1769. In 1824, at the mission’s peak, there were 1,829 neophytes, primarily Tipai-Ipai (Kumeyaay), living at the mission. Because of a shortage of food, however, many Tipai-Ipai were able to remain in their villages. San Diego Mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862. After the end of the San Diego Mission, the descendants of the Natives living here became known as Digueños.

San Diego: a city in the southernmost part of California. The home of Native peoples including the Diegueño, Luiseño, Cahuilla, and Cupeño, the area was claimed by the Spanish and named for San Diego de Alcalá de Henares, a Spanish monk, in 1602. A military post and the first of the California missions was established in San Diego in 1769, "gathering in" the Native inhabitants. Settlers started to construct the area known as Old Town in the 1820s. The new city of San Diego was laid out in 1867, and it grew rapidly due to a large naval presence, increased shipping after the completion of the Panama Canal, and the city’s temperate climate.

San Fernando: a city located in California and an enclave of Los Angeles. The area was first populated by Gabrielino (Tongva) people. In 1797, Mission San Fernando Rey de España was established nearby. The city became an organized community in 1874 and was incorporated in 1911.

San Francisco Bay: an estuary on the US Pacific coastline, stretching from San Jose north to San Rafael, California and connecting to the Pacific Ocean via the Golden Gate. The region around the Bay was home to both Ohlones and Coast Miwoks for centuries before the establishment of the Spanish presidios and Franciscan missions in the late eighteenth century. As the Bay area became more of a cultural and economic center, it expanded, and is now one of the most vibrant and influential regions on the Pacific coast.

San Francisco Mission: a mission founded in 1776 and located in San Francisco, California. Also called the Mission Delores of San Francisco de Asís, at its height (1801-21) 1,000-1,200 neophytes, mainly Ohlone, Bay Miwok, Coast Miwok, and Patwin, lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1857.

San Francisco: a California port city located on a peninsula between the San Francisco Bay and the Pacific Ocean. Lieutenant José Joaquin Moraga and the Reverend Francisco Palóu established a settlement in the area in 1776, and Mission Delores opened later the same year, "gathering in" Native peoples (the Ohlone and Miwok in particular). San Francisco boomed during the California Gold Rush. Although the city experienced a devastating earthquake in 1906, it nonetheless rapidly became a cosmopolitan center. In the 1950s, San Francisco became famous for its role in the Beat Movement and growing countercultural movement. Today, the city is known for its progressive politics and boasts one of the largest and most active LGBT communities in the world. Due to its cable cars, restored Victorian houses, and the Golden Gate Bridge (completed in 1933), it is also one of the world's most distinctive cities.

San Gabriel Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1771 and located nine miles east of downtown Los Angeles, California. Also called San Gabriel Arcángel, at the mission’s peak (1817), 1,701 Gabrielino (Tongva) neophytes lived here. The mission was secularized after Mexican independence in 1834 and returned to the Church in 1859 via a proclamation signed by President James Buchanan. The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro, California, in 1835 were not brought to San Gabriel Mission, which at this point was secularized; instead, those men and women for whom records have been found remained in the Los Angeles area.

San Gabriel: a city in Los Angeles County, California, slightly east of Los Angeles. The area is part of the Gabrielino (Tongva) homeland, and Mission San Gabriel Arcángel was founded here in 1771. The city was laid out during a land boom in the 1880s and was incorporated in 1913. San Gabriel now serves mainly as a residential community in the greater Los Angeles area.

San Joaquin County: a county in California that includes the homeland of the Miwok and Yokuts peoples. San Joaquin County was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after the nearby San Joaquin River to honor Saint Joachim. The county seat is located in the city of Stockton, and the county had a population of 728,509 in 2016.

San Joaquin River: the second longest river in California. It begins in the mountains of the Sierra Nevada and flows 330 miles to Suisun Bay, where it joins the Sacramento River. In 2014, American Rivers rated the San Joaquin River the most endangered river in the United States.

San Joaquin Valley: the southern portion of California’s Central Valley situated between the Sierra Nevada and the Coast Ranges. Part of the Miwok homeland, the valley was first settled by members of the Church of the Latter-day Saints at Stanislaus City. The valley is featured prominently in John Steinbeck’s book, The Grapes of Wrath.
San Jose: the Spanish name for Escumawash, a former Chumash village located approximately six miles from the Santa Barbara Mission in California.

San Juan Capistrano: a Catholic mission in Orange County, California. Founded in 1776, the mission "gathered in" the nearby Acjachemen (Juaneño) and Tongva (Gabrielino) peoples. By 1806, the mission had a population of 1,000 people; however, this number began to decline by 1812.  In 1833, Mexico passed a secularization act which resulted in the division of the mission's lands into parcels sold to influential California families. Today, the mission is a tourist attraction and historical site.

San Luis Obispo County: a county in California located between Santa Barbara and Monterey counties. Originally the home of the Chumash, San Luis Obispo County was first explored by Europeans in 1542, when Portuguese navigator Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo entered the Bay of San Luis Obispo. In the late eighteenth century, the Spanish established three missions in the area.

San Luis Obispo: a city in western California and the county seat of San Luis Obispo County. In Chumash territory, it was settled by the Spanish in 1772, when Mission San Luis Obispo de Tolosa was founded. A farming community grew around the mission, though severe droughts destroyed most of the farmland during the 1860s. Today, its economy relies primarily on oil, manufacturing, wineries, and tourism.

San Luis Rey Mission: also called San Luis Rey de Francía, this Catholic mission is located approximately five miles east of present-day Oceanside, California, and was founded in 1798. At the mission's peak (1825), 2,869 neophytes, Luiseño or Payomkowishum, lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Church in 1865. It was considered the most prosperous California mission.
San Marcos: the Spanish name for Mistaughchewaugh, a former Chumash village approximately twenty five miles from the Santa Barbara Mission.

San Martín: an uninhabited island in the Ensenada Municipality of Baja California. The island is located approximately six miles off the mainland near San Quintin (near the western coast of Baja California).

San Miguel Island: the most westerly of the Southern California Channel Islands. Archaeological sites place human habitation on the island as early as 11,000 BCE. Maritime Chumash communities thrived here when Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo documented San Miguel in 1542, but this ended when the population was relocated to Spanish missions on the mainland in the early nineteenth century. San Miguel then became a site for sheep ranching until overgrazing forced an end to the practice. San Miguel island is now part of the Channel Islands National Park. The US Navy also conducts operations on and near the island.

column5: San Miguel: the Spanish name for Saughpileel (also spelled Salpilel, Sa-pi-li, and Silpaleels), a former Chumash village located approximately six miles from the Santa Barbara Mission on property that became Patera Ranch.

San Nicolas Island: the most remote of the California Channel Islands. Until the mid-nineteenth century, the island was home to Island Gabrielino (Tongva) people, also known as the Nicoleños. Their name for the island is unknown, but in Tongva, the island is called Haraasnga; in Chumash, it is called Ghalas-at. The last Nicoleño to leave the island for the mainland, in 1853, became known as the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island because she lived on the island alone for eighteen years after the rest of her people had departed for the mainland. During the mid-nineteenth century, San Nicolas Island was used for sheep ranching, which led to extensive erosion. Archaeological research (and before that, treasure hunting) began on the island in the late nineteenth century, and today island artifacts are held by many institutions, including the Smithsonian, Harvard University’s Peabody Museum, the Musée de l'Homme, and the US Navy. San Nicolas Island was used for naval weapons testing beginning in 1933 and eventually became the property of the Naval Air Warfare Center, Weapons Division. San Nicolas Island is currently part of the Naval Base Ventura County.

San Nicolas Navy Station: a naval station located on California’s San Nicolas Island, which is within the 36,000-square-mile NAVAIR Sea Range in California. The station includes a missile launch platform, an observation facility, and telemetry and radar facilities.

San Pedro Bay: an inlet on the California coast that houses the Port of Long Beach and the Port of Los Angeles. The first written record of the bay appeared in 1542, when explorer Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo designated it the "Bay of Smokes" -- its smoky appearance resulted from the large quantity of fires built by Gabrielino (Tongva) inhabitants of the area. The bay became one of the busiest ports in the world after the United States Congress declared it the official port of the City of Los Angeles.

San Pedro: a port, formerly a separate city, that is now the main unit of the Port of Los Angeles. Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo arrived in this Gabrielino (Tongva) territory in 1542, and the site was used as a trading post by the Spanish. The city of San Pedro was laid out in 1882 and incorporated in 1888. It was annexed by Los Angeles in 1909 and now boasts one of the world’s largest manmade harbors.

San Rafael Mission: a Catholic mission located fifteen miles north of San Francisco. Also called San Rafael Arcángel, it was founded in 1817 as a hospital asistencia and was granted full mission status in 1822. At the mission's peak (1826), it had 1,051 neophytes, mostly Coast Miwok and Pomo. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Church in 1855. San Rafael Mission was used as a military headquarters during the Mexican-American War.

San Simeon: a town on the coast of San Luis Obispo County, California. Traditionally the homeland of the Chumash people, the settlement of San Simeon began after Mission San Miguel was secularized and divided into three ranchos. José de Jesús Pico bought the Rancho Piedras Blancas in 1840 and sold part of it to George Hearst in 1865. George’s son, William Randolph Hearst, constructed the famous Hearst Castle, which remains an important tourist attraction in the area today.

San Ynez Mission: a Catholic mission located in the Santa Inéz Valley on the eastern edge of Solvang, California. Also called also called Santa Inés Virgen y Martír or simply Santa Inés, this mission was founded in 1804. At the mission's peak (1815), 768 neophytes, mostly Chumash people, lived here. The largest Native uprising during the mission era began here in 1824. The mission was secularized in 1836 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862.

Sandwich Islands: the name given in 1778 to what is now known as the Hawaiian Islands.  British explorer James Cook named the islands in honor of his patron John Montagu, fourth Earl of Sandwich. The Hawaiian Islands became a central point of trade in the Pacific in the nineteenth century, with American and British merchant houses establishing offices in Honolulu in the 1820s and 30s.

Santa Ana Army Air Base: an air base located in Orange County, California, that employed around 26,000 personnel at its height. The air base, which began construction in 1941, was primarily used as a classification and pre-flight training site during World War II. It was deactivated in 1946.

Santa Ana: a city located in Orange County, California, at the base of the Santa Ana Mountains. The area was part of the territory of the Acjachemen (Juaneño) and Tongva (Gabrielino) peoples; they were "gathered in" to the San Juan Capistrano Mission during the Spanish era. In 1801, the area constituting present-day Santa Ana became part of a Mexican land grant: the Rancho Santiago de Santa Ana. Santa Ana was laid out as a city in 1869 and incorporated in 1886.
Santa Barbara Channel: a strip of the Pacific Ocean between the mainland of California (south of Santa Barbara and west of Ventura and Port Hueneme) and the Channel Islands.

Santa Barbara County: a county in Southern California comprised of Santa Barbara and surrounding areas, including cities such as Carpinteria, Summerland, Montecito, Goleta, Lompoc, and Santa Maria. Santa Barbara County is part of the Chumash homeland; it had a population of 423,895 in 2010.

Santa Barbara Island: the smallest of the Southern California Channel Islands. While it could not sustain permanent settlements, the Chumash and Gabrielino (Tongva) people visited the island seasonally as early as 4,000 years ago. Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo first documented the island in 1542. After the Mexican-American War, the US government acquired ownership of the island and began leasing it to individuals for agricultural and recreational uses beginning in 1900. The island is now a part of the Channel Islands National Park.

column5: Santa Barbara Mission Cemetery: a burial ground established in 1789 that holds the remains of many indigenous people brought to Santa Barbara Mission, including an estimated 4,000 Chumash.  The specific site of the Lone Woman's burial is unknown, as Indian graves were unmarked.

Santa Barbara Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1786 near the Chumash village of Xana’yan and located in Santa Barbara, California. It was the tenth mission founded by the Franciscans in California and is known as the Queen of the Missions because of its beauty. At the mission's peak (1803), 1,792 neophytes, Chumash people (the Barbareño and the Canaleño), lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1865. In 1853, the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island was conditionally baptized and buried at the mission. She was not reunited with her people at the mission, as they had been brought to Los Angeles. Today the Santa Barbara Mission is owned and operated by the Franciscan order, serving as both a church and a museum.

column4: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History
column5: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History: a museum showcasing exhibits relating to geology, paleontology, archaeology, anthropology, and biology. William Leon Dawson founded the museum in 1916 as the Museum of Comparative Oology. It moved to its present location in 1922 and opened the Sea Center on Stearns Wharf in 1987.

Santa Barbara: a city in California named for the patron saint of mariners. Part of the Chumash homeland, it is located ninety-seven miles northwest of Los Angeles at the base of the Santa Ynez Mountains. Due to its proximity to the mountains, it has a mild climate year-round. A Spanish military outpost was established in Santa Barbara in 1782, and the Santa Barbara Mission, which served as the western headquarters of the Franciscan order, was established in 1786. The Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island was brought to Santa Barbara in 1853, and she was baptized at this mission (the baptism was conditional since no one who spoke her language fluently could be found. The rest of the people removed from San Nicolas Island had been brought to San Pedro in 1835). In the late 1800s, Santa Barbara became known as a ranching and resort town with an economy based on tourism, cattle, citrus fruits, and petroleum. The city was devastated by an earthquake in 1925, and many Victorian-era houses burned. Today, Santa Barbara is an affluent city that boasts many Spanish Colonial buildings, as local codes require adobe-style architecture in the downtown area.

Santa Catalina Island: the largest of the Southern California Channel Islands. Inhabited since around 7000 BCE, it was first documented by Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo in 1542, during his exploration of the California coast. After the Gabrielino (Tongva) people were relocated to Spanish missions on the mainland at the beginning of the nineteenth century, the island was used for hunting, gold mining, and ranching. Ownership of the island was consolidated in the late nineteenth century when the Banning Brothers created the Santa Catalina Island Company and built a resort. In 1912, the Wrigley family (of chewing gum fame) purchased the island in its entirety. About twenty percent of the island remains a resort today, but the Wrigleys donated 42,000 acres to the Catalina Island Conservancy in 1975, and much of the island is now protected.

column5: Santa Clara County: a county in California that includes the homeland of the Costanoan people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after the nearby Mission Santa Clara, which was founded in 1777. The county seat is located in the city of San Jose, and the county had a population of 1,915,407 in 2016.

Santa Clara River: a river that begins in Acton, California, and flows approximately 100 miles through Los Angeles and Ventura counties to the Pacific Ocean. In 2005, American Rivers rated the Santa Clara River as one of the top ten most endangered rivers in the United States.

Santa Cruz County: a county in California that is part of the Costanoan people’s homeland. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and originally named Branciforte County after a nearby Spanish pueblo established in 1797. The county seat is located in the city of Santa Cruz, and the county had a population of 274,594 in 2016.

Santa Cruz Island: the largest of the Channel Islands located off the southern coast of California; it was first inhabited around 12,000 BCE. Sebastián Vizcaíno documented the island in 1602, and beginning in the late eighteenth century, the Spanish removed the Santa Cruz Island Chumash to Mission San Buenaventura, across the channel; the last Chumash left the island in 1822. Throughout this period, Santa Cruz was popular with otter hunters and with smugglers, who used the aptly named Smuggler’s Cove, on the east coast, as a hiding place for their goods. Santa Cruz was used for sheep ranching in the mid nineteenth century. The island is now a part of the Channel Islands National Park and Nature Conservancy.

Santa Cruz: a city on the central coast of California and part of the Ohlone (Costanoan) homeland. Santa Cruz was claimed by the Spanish in 1769 and given its current name, which translates as “holy cross.” In 1791, a Franciscan mission was established in the area, the twelfth one on the California coast. Santa Cruz became an incorporated town in 1866, and by the early twentieth century it was a city finding success in logging, fishing, and agriculture. Today, the city is known for its seaside boardwalk, beach resorts, and recreation such as surfing and golfing.

Santa Monica: a city located in Los Angeles County, California, in the homeland of the Gabrielino (Tongva) people. Santa Monica was founded in 1875 and was incorporated in 1886. Although originally envisioned as a port city, Santa Monica became known as a vacation destination and is famous for its pier.

Santa Paula: a city in Ventura County, California. Located in Santa Clara River Valley, it is surrounded by abundant citrus groves. In the late nineteenth century, the Union Oil Company of California built its headquarters here, and the city was a center for the petroleum industry. In 2015, Santa Paula had a population of approximately 30,000.

Santa Rosa Island: one of the eight Southern California Channel Islands. The oldest known evidence of human habitation in North America has been found on the island: fossil human remains dating to 13,000 BCE. More recently, the island was occupied by the Chumash, and Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo documented his encounter with Santa Rosa islanders during his exploration of the California coast in 1542. The Spanish began removing the Island Chumash to Catholic missions on the mainland in the early nineteenth century, with the last Native leaving Santa Rosa in 1817. During the nineteenth century, the island was used for sheep and cattle ranching. Today, Santa Rosa is part of the Channel Islands National Park.
