column5: Santa Rosa: a city located in Sonoma County, California, home to the Pomo, Miwok, and Wappo peoples. Santa Rosa was founded in 1833 and incorporated in 1868. The city was the home of Fountain Grove, a utopian experiment established in 1885.
Santa Ynez Mountains: one of the only east-west trending mountain ranges in the United States, located in Southern California’s Santa Barbara and Ventura Counties.

Santa Ynez Valley: the valley between the Santa Ynez and San Rafael Mountains, in Santa Barbara County; it is in the Chumash homeland. Today the valley, which boasts a thriving industry in wine and tourism, is home to six towns:  Ballard, Buellton, Los Alamos, Los Olivos, Santa Ynez, and Solvang.

Santa Ynez: a town in the Santa Ynez Valley of California. Settlers began arriving in this area, which is part of Chumash territory, in 1881. The town experienced a brief population boom in the 1880s in anticipation of the railroad's arrival, but when the Southern Pacific Railroad decided not to build a connection through the town, Santa Ynez faded back into relative obscurity.
Saticoy: a census-designated area located in Ventura County, California, and home of the Chumash people. In 2016, the area had an estimated population of 1,101.

Sausalito: a city in Marin County, California. Originally the home of Coast Miwok Natives, it became a Mexican land grant, Rancho Sausalito. William Richardson, the land grantee, founded the town in 1838 and sold the land to Charles Botts after the Gold Rush in 1849. Today, Sausalito is primarily a bedroom community of San Francisco.

Shasta County: a county in northern California that encompasses the homeland of the Achomawi, Atsugewi, Okwanuchu, Wintu, and Yana peoples. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850. The county seat is located in the city of Redding, and the county had a population of 178,795 in 2016.

Sibagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at the site of Mission San Gabriel in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Tobiscagna, Toviseanga, Tobiscanga, Tobizcanga, Tuvasak, Toviscanga, and Toviseanga.

Sierra County: a county in northern California that includes the homeland of the Maidu and Washoe peoples. Sierra County was incorporated on April 16, 1852, and is named after the nearby Sierra Nevada mountain range. The county seat is located in the town of Downieville, and the county had a population of 3,166 in 2016.

Sierra Nevada: a mountain range extending over 250 miles along California’s eastern edge. Part of the homeland of the Yokut, Sierra Miwok, Maidu, Owens Valley Paiute, and Washoe, the Sierra Nevada range experienced an influx of settlers during the Gold Rush of 1849. Today, the mountain range serves as an important source of water and power for much of California’s population.

Sisitcanogna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located in the San Gabriel Valley at Pear Orchard in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Sisitkanonga, Sisitcanonga, and Sisitkanogna.

Siskiyou County: a county in northern California that includes the homeland of the Karuk, Modoc, Wintu, Achomawi, and Klamath peoples. It was incorporated on March 22, 1852, and is named after the nearby Siskiyou mountain range. The county seat is located in the city of Yreka, and the county had a population of 44,563 in 2016.

Sisters School: a school located in Santa Barbara, California, founded in 1858 and operated by the Sisters of Charity of St. Vincent de Paul. The school was initially located near Carrillo and State Streets, where General Fremont’s headquarters were located. It later moved to 925 De La Vina Street, where it remained until the 1925 earthquake.
Sitka: the capital of Russian America, known by the Russians as New Archangel. It is part of the homeland of the Tlingit people. Today, Sitka is a small city and borough located near Juneau.

Six Bull River: a variant name for the Neosho River, a tributary of the Arkansas River. It is about 460 miles and flows through eastern Kansas and northeastern Oklahoma, meeting the Arkansas River around Muskogee. Other names for the Neosho River have included Grand River, Osage River, and Rio Blanco.

Smithsonian Institution: a museum and research complex headquartered at the National Mall in Washington, DC, encompassing nineteen museums, nine research centers, and the National Zoo. The institution was founded at the bequest of James Smithson, a British scientist who died in 1829 and never visited the United States.
Smuggler’s Cove: a bay on San Clemente Island in California. Bootleggers and smugglers used the remote cove to hide their goods, but only when more convenient hiding spots were unavailable.
Smuggler’s Cove: a bay on Santa Cruz Island in California. The cove was a popular spot for bootleggers and smugglers to hide their goods because the area was isolated yet still accessible.
Sonagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located at White’s Ranch in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Sonagno and Sonanga.

Sonoma County: a county in California that makes up the homeland of the Pomo, Coast Miwok, and Wappo people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after a Chocuyen word meaning either "valley of the moon" or "land or tribe of the Chief Nose." The county seat is located in the city of Santa Rosa, and the county had a population of 500,487 in 2016.

Sonora: a city located in California in the foothills of the Sierra Nevada. First populated by Me-Wuk Natives, the area grew rapidly during the Gold Rush. The city was incorporated in 1851 and had a reputation as a dangerous frontier town. Sonora was chosen as one of California’s first "Main Street" cities in 1986.

South of France: a region of France extending from the Spanish border to the Italian border consisting of Provence, the Alps, Cote d’Azur, and Languedoc-Roussillon. Southern France (especially the French Riviera) is a popular tourist destination.

Southern Channel Islands: San Clemente, San Nicolas, Santa Barbara, and Santa Catalina lie west of the San Pedro Channel and form the Santa Catalina group. The Gabrielino (Tongva), a maritime people, lived on the larger of the three islands, San Clemente, San Nicolas, and Santa Catalina, until the Spanish brought them to the mainland, either as a direct result of the mission system or as an effect of depopulation in the wake of European arrival. The last Gabrielino (Tongva) to live on one of the Southern Channel Islands, the Lone Woman of San Nicolas, removed to the mainland in 1853.

Spain: a country located in the Iberian Peninsula of southwestern Europe. After Christopher Columbus reached the Americas in 1492, Spanish conquistadors laid claim to the land, often forcibly converting the Native peoples and pressing them into service. The Spanish continued this practice in what was eventually to become the US state of California, the northern reaches of their empire.  Spain lost much of its overseas possessions, including the modern day country of Mexico and US state of California, in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

St. Louis: a city located on the western bank of the Mississippi River in Missouri. The region of St. Louis was inhabited by the Mound Builders of the Mississippian culture until the fourteenth century. The Missouri and Osage peoples dominated the region when the French established St. Louis as a fur trading post in 1764. Spain gained control of the territory in 1768, and though it was returned to France in 1800, St. Louis was run by Spanish governors until the Louisiana Purchase (1803), which transferred the land to the United States. St. Louis was the departure site for the Lewis and Clark expedition the following year. The town's population grew and St. Louis became an important center for commerce and trade, especially the fur industry. It was incorporated as a city in 1823. Following waves of immigration and the hosting of the 1904 Olympic Games, the population peaked at 800,000 in 1950, and then fell dramatically due to suburbanization. In 1978, Scott O’Dell attended the Catholic Library Association Convention in St. Louis to accept the Regina Medal Award.

St. Mary’s Church: a Catholic cathedral located in San Francisco on the corner of California Street and Dupont (later Grant Avenue). The cornerstone for St. Mary's Church was laid in 1853 and the cathedral was dedicated at Christmas Mass in 1854. When neighborhood demographics changed in the 1880s, the church was moved. A new Cathedral of St. Mary of the Assumption was dedicated in January 1891 and the former cathedral on California Street, which became known as "Old Saint Mary's," became a Chinese Mission operated by the Paulists, a missionary society. Old Saint Mary’s was restored and rededicated in 1909, following damage in the 1906 earthquake.

Stanislaus River: a California river that begins in the foothills of the Sierra Nevada mountain chain and flows sixty-five miles through San Joaquin, Stanislaus, Calaveras, and Tuolumne counties to the San Joaquin River. In 1806, Gabriel Moraga named the river after Our Lady of Guadalupe, but the river was later renamed to honor Estanislao, a Native leader.

State Street: a street in Santa Barbara, named when California was celebrating its admittance to the Union as a state. Salisbury Haley conducted a survey of the city after statehood, and an attempt was made to restructure the city on a grid system. In Haley's survey, Carrillo and State Streets were the only two listed as being 80-feet-wide rather than 60-feet-wide.
Steglitz-Berlin: a neighborhood in southwest Berlin, Germany. During the Cold War, this neighborhood was part of West Germany.

Stockton: a city located in California along the San Joaquin River. First occupied by the Yatchicumne, the area became part of a Mexican land grant in 1844. The city was founded as Tuleburg in 1849, and its population expanded greatly during the Gold Rush. The city was renamed and incorporated in 1850.

Stoneapple Farm: country home in Julian, California, where Scott O'Dell wrote Island of the Blue Dolphins, among other books. The remodeled apple storage and packing house was built out of stone in the late 19th century and converted into a residential home in the 1940s. After Scott O'Dell and his wife Dorsa separated, Dorsa continued to live at Stoneapple Farm until her death in 2008. The property is now owned by Dorsa O'Dell's niece and it serves as a writer's cottage.
Suagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Cerritos or Palos Verdes in Los Angeles County, California. Alternate spellings include Shuavit, Suanga, Suangna, and Soabit.

Sullivan County: a county in eastern Tennessee, bordering Virginia. Sullivan County’s Long Island is a sacred spot for the Cherokee. Settlers arrived in the late 1760s, and the county was established in 1780. Today, Kingsport and Bristol, two cities in Tennessee’s Tri-Cities region, are located in Sullivan County.

Sutter County: a county in California that encompasses the homeland of the Maidu people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after General John Augustus Sutter, a Swiss immigrant who obtained a land grant from the Mexican government and settled present-day Sacramento. The county seat is located in Yuba City, and the county had a population of 97,701 in 2016.

Taboga: an island in the Bay of Panama, approximately eleven miles south of Panama City. Isla Taboga is approximately two miles long and one mile wide and is renowned for its mangoes and pineapples. The island houses Iglesia San Pedro, which is allegedly the Western Hemisphere’s second-oldest church.

Tehama County: a county in northern California that is the traditional homeland of the Nomlaki (also spelled Nomi Lackee) people. Tehama County was incorporated on April 9, 1856, and is named after the City of Tehama. The county seat is located in the city of Red Bluff, and the county had a population of 63,975 in 2016.

Temple of the Sun: a Gabrielino (Tongva) religious complex on Santa Catalina Island, California. It consisted of a collection of outdoor altars, large stones, and ceremonial burial sites. The complex has long since vanished, possibly due to earthquakes, landslides, or heavy rains.

Tennessee: the sixteenth state of the United States, which it joined on June 1, 1796. Tennessee includes the homeland of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Creek, Koasati, Quapaw, Shawnee, and Yuchi peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, electrical power, enriched uranium production, music, automobile manufacturing, mining, tourism, and horse breeding.

Terminal Island: a largely artificial island in San Pedro Bay, off the coast of southern California. To improve the bay’s harbor infrastructure, a long rock jetty was built between the Bay’s Deadman’s Island and nearby Rattlesnake Island to create a shipping channel between the islands and the mainland. The breakwater caused the reshaping of Rattlesnake Island and the disintegration of most of Deadman’s Island. In 1891, the Terminal Company bought Rattlesnake Island and changed its name to Terminal Island. Author Scott O’Dell was born here in 1898. What was left of Deadman’s Island was removed completely in 1928 and added on to Terminal Island. Terminal Island was a large Japanese fishing village until Executive Order 9066 in 1942 led to the internment of Japanese Americans. Today, Terminal Island is split between the city of Long Beach and the city of Los Angeles. It houses a Federal Correction Institution and is an industrial port.

Texas: the twenty-eighth state of the United States, which it joined on December 29, 1845. Texas is the homeland of the Apache, Bidai, Coahuiltecan, Carrizo, Caddo, Comanche, Jumano, Suma, Piro, Karankawa, Kiowa, Kitsai, Tawakoni, Tonkawa, and Wichita peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, oil and natural gas extraction, steel production, banking, tourism, and insurance.

Thailand: a country in southeastern Asia bordered by Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, and Malaysia. Originally settled by Tai-speaking peoples in the eleventh century, Thailand became a unified kingdom in the mid-fourteenth century. Thailand, which was called Siam until 1939, is the only southeast Asian country that was never colonized by Europeans. The country was an absolute monarchy until 1932, when a bloodless revolution created a constitutional monarchy. Since 2005, Thailand has experienced a military coup and a variety of major political protests.

the Lewis and Henry’s Forks: Lewis Fork is a variant name for the 1078-mile Snake River, which heads in the Grand Tetons of Wyoming and flows to Washington’s Columbia River. Henry’s Fork is a 127-mile tributary of the Snake River located in Idaho. Many Native groups, such as the , Blackfeet, and Shoshone, lived on or near the Henry’s Fork watershed.

Thomas Hope's Land, "Las Positas": land purchased by Thomas Hope, an Irish sheep farmer, in 1861 and part of the homeland of the Cieneguitas tribe. Also known as Hope Ranch, the Southern Pacific Railroad bought the land in 1876, but residential development did not commence on the property until 1923, when Harold Chase bought the land. It is now a suburb of Santa Barbara, California.
Tibihagna: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near Bixby Knolls and Lakewood, California. Alternate spellings include Tibahanga, Tibahagna, and Tibaha.

Tiryns: a prehistoric city located in present-day Argolis, Greece. By the Early Bronze Age, the area was occupied by an agricultural pre-Greek civilization. The city became a center of Mycenaean culture and was destroyed by Argos around 468 BCE. By the second century CE, the area was deserted.
Tomales Bay: a bay in Marin County, California, that is part of the Coast Miwok homeland. It is also known as Estero Americano, Estero De Tamales, and Rio Grande de San Sebasitian.
Toybipet: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) settlement located in Los Angeles County, California, on a site later known as San Jose. It was also known as Sibapot and Toibi.

Trinity County: a county in northern California that is the part of the homeland of the Wintu people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is termed after the Trinity River, which Major Pearson B. Reading named because he mistakenly believed that it emptied into Trinidad Bay. The county seat is located in the town of Weaverville, and the county had a population of 13,583 in 2016.

Tulare County: a county in California that is part of the homeland of the Yokuts. Tulare County was incorporated on April 20, 1852, and is named after the Tulare Lake, which Commandante Fages deemed "Los Tules" in 1772. The county seat is located in the city of Visalia, and the county had a population of 465,013 in 2016.

Tuolumne County: a county in California that is part of the homeland of the Me-Wuk people. It was incorporated on February 18, 1850, and is named after a Native word meaning "many stone houses," "the land of mountain lions," or "straight up steep." The county seat is located in the city of Sonora, and the county had a population of 54,511 in 2016.

Tuolumne River: a river in California that begins in Yosemite National Park and travels through the Tuolumne Meadows and Don Pedro Reservoir before flowing into the San Joaquin River. The Tuolumne River harbors 34 endemic species, including the Yosemite toad and the Dwarf downingia.

Tzintzuntzan: the capital of the Tarascan Empire, which flourished in western Mexico from 1350 to 1522 CE. Tzintzuntzan was located on the northeastern part of Lake Pátzcuaro and had a population of 35,000 by 1522. It served as the commercial, governmental, and religious center of the Tarascan civilization.

Ueckermünde: a seaport town in northeastern Germany founded in the Middle Ages by the Ukrani, a Wendish tribe. Ueckermünde became the district town in 1818, a position it held until 1994. After World War II, Ueckermünde became part of East Germany and was recognized as an official holiday resort.

Umpqua River: a river that begins nears Roseburg, Oregon, and flows approximately 111 miles through Douglas County to Winchester Bay, where it enters the Pacific Ocean. Several Native peoples, including the Umpqua, Molala, and Kalapuyan, lived on or near the river.

Valdez Harbor: refers to a location on the northwestern part of Santa Cruz Island also known as Valdez Cave and Cueva Valdez. Cueva Valdez is famous for its large cave and has been a favorite picnicking and camping destination since the early twentieth century.

Valparaiso: a Chilean city located on the Pacific Ocean. Originally occupied by the Changos, the area was first explored by Europeans in 1536, when Juan de Saavedra founded the city. It became an important stopping point in the Pacific maritime trade. The city experienced major earthquakes in 1906, 1971, 1985, and 2010.

Ventura County Navy Station: the California naval station more commonly known as Naval Base Ventura County (NBVC). NBVC, a naval installation encompassing Point Mugu, San Nicolas Island, and Port Hueneme, was created in 2000 when the commands of Point Mugu and Port Hueneme were consolidated.

Ventura County: a county in Southern California encompassing Los Padres National Forest, extensive farmland, and ten cities, including Ventura, Thousand Oaks, and Simi Valley (lands part of the Chumash homeland). The county was first settled by Europeans in 1782, when Mission San Buenaventura was constructed. Ventura County was officially formed in 1873, when it split from Santa Barbara County. Its county seat is Ventura and had a population of approximately 850,000 in 2016.

Ventura: a city in Southern California located between Malibu and Santa Barbara and within Chumash territory. Mission San Buenaventura was founded here in 1782, and the city, which became a lemon-growing center, was incorporated in 1866. It became the county seat in 1873 when the Santa Barbara and Ventura counties split. The city of Ventura grew rapidly due to an oil strike in 1914.

Village: a Native village (or in Spanish, rancheria) located on Aliso Street, which was the road between Los Angeles and Mission San Gabriel. In the early nineteenth century, it was populated by Gabrielinos (Tongva) who were employed as farm laborers in the nearby vineyards and orchards of white settlers.

Virginia: the tenth state of the United States, which it officially joined on June 25, 1788. Virginia is the home of the Catawba, Cherokee, Croatoan, Powhatan, Tuscarora, Tutelo, Saponi, and Yuchi peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, mining, tourism, lumber, and governmental and naval activities.

Warner's Ranch: a 44,322-acre Mexican land grant in San Diego County, California and part of the Cupeño homeland. During the Spanish mission period, the valley served as a cattle grazing area for Mission San Diego and Mission San Luis Rey. The first land grant in the valley was made in 1836 to Silvestre de la Portilla and called Rancho Valle de San José. In 1840, José Antonio Pico was granted the part of the valley called Agua Caliente (for its hot mineral springs); he named it Rancho San José del Valle. Cupeño resistance to these landholdings ultimately led both ranchers to abandon the property; as a result, Juan Jose Warner was able to acquire the entire valley in 1844. His Warner's Ranch served as a way station for those traveling to California by the Gila Trail.  In 1857, the portion of Warner's Ranch that had been Rancho Agua Caliente land was re-awarded to an earlier claimant, and in 1861, Warner lost the remaining portion of Warner’s Ranch after falling into debt.

Washington Territory: an area created by an act of the US Congress in 1853. The region was named for George Washington and encompassed the homeland of the Northwest Coast and Plateau people. It comprised the present-day state of Washington and parts of Montana and Idaho.

Washington, DC: the capital of the United States of America, situated between the states of Maryland and Virginia. On July 16, 1790, Congress passed a law that provided for the selection of ten square miles of land along the Potomac River to become the nation's capital. Today, it is both a political and cultural center for the country, housing many museums, memorials, and national landmarks.

West Indies: a group of islands in the Atlantic Ocean stretching over 2,000 miles and separating the Caribbean Sea from the Gulf of Mexico. The West Indies is comprised of several island groups, including the Greater Antilles, the Lesser Antilles, and the Bahamas. When Christopher Columbus became the first European to explore the area in 1492, the islands were primarily occupied by Carib and Taino people. Believing that he had reached Asia, Columbus named the islands the Indies. However, when the mistake was discovered, Spain renamed them the West Indies to distinguish this group from the Spice Islands or East Indies, now called Indonesia.

West Virginia: the thirty-fifth state of the United States, which it joined on June 20, 1863, after splitting with Virginia. The land comprising the state is home to the Cherokee, Shawnee, Tutelo, and Saponi peoples. Today, West Virginia's primary industries are coal mining, agriculture, chemical and glass manufacturing, and tourism.

Whittier Public Library: a library system located within the city of Whittier, California, in Los Angeles County. The library was founded in 1900, and a building was financed in 1907 by a Carnegie grant. The library’s second and only branch opened in 1968.

Wichita: a city in Sedgwick County, Kansas, located on the Arkansas River. Originally the site of a Wichita village, the town began as a trading post founded by J. R. Mead in 1864. Due to agricultural settlement, the town grew rapidly and was incorporated in 1871.
Worthington: a city in southwest Minnesota. It was established in 1871 as a Union Pacific Railway line stop between Sioux City and St. Paul.

Wyoming: the forty-fourth state of the United States, which it officially joined on July 10, 1890. Wyoming is the home of the Arapaho, Cheyenne, Crow, Shoshone, and Ute peoples. Today, its primary industries are agriculture, mining, tourism, and oil and natural gas extraction.
Xabaagua: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and later listed in California mission documents.

Xucu: a Chumash village listed in the notes of the Cabrillo exploration of the United States' western coastline (1542-43) and later listed in California mission documents. The contemporary county of Ventura currently includes the site of this former village.

Xugua: a Chumash village on Santa Cruz Island, the largest of the California Channel Islands. Also spelled Xuga, the Chumash people lived on the island for thousands of years and developed a highly complex society dependent on the sea, specialized craft, and mainland trade. Although the Chumash did not have contact with Europeans until the sixteenth century, by 1822 all Chumash people had been relocated to the mainland.
Yanga: a former Gabrielino (Tongva) village located near San Fernando Mission (in or near the present-day city of Los Angeles, California). It was also known as Saway-yanga.

Yanonali Street: a street located in Santa Barbara and named after the Chumash chief Yanonali. Chief Yanonali was the leader of one of the largest Chumash villages at the time Spaniards built the Santa Barbara Presidio. Yanonali converted to Christianity at the Santa Barbara Mission.

Yellowstone River: a river that begins in Wyoming and flows approximately 692 miles through Yellowstone Lake and Montana. The river joins the Missouri River near Buford, North Dakota. Yellowstone River was first explored by non-Native people during the expedition of Lewis and Clark in 1806.
Yerba Buena Island: a small island located in the San Francisco Bay west of Oakland and east of the Golden Gate. It was formerly called Goat Island.

Yolo County: Yolo County was incorporated in 1850, making it one of the first counties in the state of California. The name 'Yolo' is of Patwin origin, but the exact meaning is disputed. It may be a deviation of the tribal name "Yoloy," the chief name "Yodo," or the community name "Yodoi."

column5: Yosemite Valley: a glacial valley in the western Sierra Nevada mountains that is home to the Ahwaheechee people. Members of Joseph Walker's expedition were likely the first European-Americans to see Yosemite, which attracted fortune-seekers when gold was discovered in the early 1850s. Significant violence between miners and Natives ensued. The Valley had already become a popular tourist destination by the late nineteenth century, though a federal bill protected the area as early as 1864 and in 1890 Yosemite became a national park.

column5: Yuba County: a county located in Northern California named for the Yuba River. While it was incorporated in 1850, the original territory was later reapportioned for the creation of the Nevada and Sierra counties that border it today. The county seat, Marysville, sits along the Yuba River and was a mining town.

Yuba River: a watershed marked by its initial point in Sierra County, California, and confluence into the Feather River of Yuba County, California. The Yuba River was significant during the Gold Rush, with some of the richest claims found along its path. Captain John Sutter gave the river its name, but the origin is disputed. It may be a variation of 'Yubu,' a Maidu village in the area, or it may be a form of the Spanish word for grapes ("'uva"), as the fruit originally grew along the river's banks.

column5: Zenith City: a nickname for Santa Barbara, California. A local nineteenth-century literary society was named the Zenith City Circle in honor of this moniker.
